content:
# Genesis Chapter 22 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 12r–12v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_022_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_022_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 17–18 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** After these things God tested Abraham and said to him, "Abraham!" And he said, "Here I am."

**2** He said, "Take your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love, and go to the land of Moriah, and offer him there as a burnt offering on one of the mountains of which I shall tell you."

**3** So Abraham rose early in the morning, saddled his donkey, and took two of his young men with him, and his son Isaac. And he split the wood for the burnt offering and arose and went to the place of which God had told him.

**4** On the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes and saw the place from afar.

**5** Then Abraham said to his young men, "Stay here with the donkey; I and the boy will go over there and worship and come back to you."

**6** And Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son. And he took in his hand the fire and the knife. So they went both of them together.

**7** And Isaac said to his father Abraham, "My father!" And he said, "Here I am, my son." He said, "Behold, the fire and the wood, but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?"

**8** Abraham said, "God will provide for himself the lamb for a burnt offering, my son." So they went both of them together.

**9** When they came to the place of which God had told him, Abraham built the altar there and laid the wood in order. And he bound Isaac his son and laid him on the altar, on top of the wood.

**10** Then Abraham reached out his hand and took the knife to slaughter his son.

**11** But the angel of the LORD called to him from heaven and said, "Abraham, Abraham!" And he said, "Here I am."

**12** He said, "Do not lay your hand on the boy or do anything to him, for now I know that you fear God, seeing you have not withheld your son, your only son, from me."

**13** And Abraham lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, behind him was a ram, caught in a thicket by his horns. And Abraham went and took the ram and offered it up as a burnt offering instead of his son.

**14** So Abraham called the name of that place, "The LORD will provide"; as it is said to this day, "On the mount of the LORD it shall be provided."

**15** And the angel of the LORD called to Abraham a second time from heaven

**16** and said, "By myself I have sworn, declares the LORD, because you have done this thing and have not withheld your son, your only son,

**17** I will surely bless you, and I will surely multiply your offspring as the stars of heaven and as the sand that is on the seashore. And your offspring shall possess the gate of his enemies,

**18** and in your offspring all the nations of the earth shall be blessed, because you have obeyed my voice."

**19** So Abraham returned to his young men, and they arose and went together to Beer-sheba. And Abraham lived at Beer-sheba.

**20** Now after these things it was told to Abraham, "Behold, Milcah also has borne children to your brother Nahor:

**21** Uz his firstborn, Buz his brother, Kemuel the father of Aram,

**22** Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel."

**23** Bethuel fathered Rebekah. These eight Milcah bore to Nahor, Abraham's brother.

**24** Moreover, his concubine, whose name was Reumah, bore Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, and Maacah.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 22 spans Folios 12r–12v.

   **Folio 12r** (GEN_022_page_001.jpg): The recto of Folio 12. The right-hand column closes Genesis 21 (the Beer-sheba tamarisk planting and sojourn notice), and the central and left columns carry Genesis 22 from its opening petuchah paragraph marker through approximately verse 13–14. The Hebrew text is fully legible with Masoretic pointing (niqqud), cantillation marks (te'amim), and the characteristic three-column Tiberian layout. The petuchah paragraph division marking the transition from Genesis 21 to 22 is clearly visible, establishing the narrative break that opens the Aqedah. Verses 1 through approximately 13 are legible in the central and left columns of this folio.

   **Folio 12v** (GEN_022_page_002.jpg): The verso of Folio 12. This folio carries Genesis 22:14 through 22:24 — the second angelic oracle from heaven (vv. 15–18), the divine oath sworn by YHWH himself, the renewed blessing with its promises of innumerable offspring and possession of the enemies' gate, the return to Beer-sheba (v. 19), and the Nahor genealogy that closes the chapter by introducing Rebekah (vv. 20–24). The text continues into Genesis 23 on this same folio. The Masoretic paragraph divisions are visible, as is the setumah marking before the genealogical section. Note: the image of folio 12v is reproduced in inverted orientation; the text itself is fully legible once the orientation is corrected.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Page 17 (GEN_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries Genesis 22 in the LXX tradition, with the continuous uncial script in three columns across the page. The characteristic Vaticanus hand — a fine, regular uncial without word divisions — is clearly legible throughout. Page 18 (GEN_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the conclusion of Genesis 22 and the opening of Genesis 23, with "CAP. XXV" visible as a section marker at the top and a further chapter division marker appearing toward the lower right margin. The Vaticanus text of Genesis 22 (LXX) was read against the MT at every point of translation decision.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. וְהָאֱלֹהִים נִסָּה אֶת אַבְרָהָם — "God Tested Abraham" (v. 1) — The Verb נִסָּה**

The chapter opens with the narrator's omniscient announcement — a detail withheld from Abraham, and from Isaac, throughout the narrative:

וְהָאֱלֹהִים נִסָּה אֶת אַבְרָהָם
(veha'Elohim nissah et Avraham)
"God tested Abraham"

The verb נִסָּה (nissah, Piel of נָסָה, "to test, to prove, to try") is the key term for the reader's theological orientation to the entire narrative. Without this opening disclosure, the chapter would read as divine cruelty or capriciousness. The narrator grants the reader a knowledge Abraham does not possess: this is a test. It is not a temptation to sin (the verb נָסָה is never used of satanic temptation in the OT), but a divine proving of character — the same verb used for God's testing of Israel at Massah (Exodus 17:2) and in the wilderness (Deuteronomy 8:2, 16). The formal translation renders "tested" (ESV, NASB, KJV) rather than "tried" or "put to the proof," preserving both the personal and the probative force of the term.

The article before Elohim — הָאֱלֹהִים (ha'Elohim, "the God" or "God") — appears with unusual frequency throughout the Aqedah (vv. 1, 3, 8, 9, 12) and is characteristic of this narrative's solemn, formal register. The formal translation renders simply "God" throughout (as is standard in English).

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders: ὁ θεὸς ἐπείραζεν τὸν Αβρααμ (ho theos epeiradzen ton Abraam, "God was testing/tempting Abraham"), using the verb πειράζω (peiradzo), which in later Greek can carry the sense of "to tempt." The NT distinction between God "testing" (πειράζω in the sense of probation) and "tempting to evil" (same verb, different sense) is addressed by James 1:13 — "God cannot be tempted with evil, and he himself tempts no one" — which presupposes the Genesis 22 narrative and the semantic range of the term. The formal translation retains "tested" to preserve the OT theological register.

**2. אֶת בִּנְךָ אֶת יְחִידְךָ אֲשֶׁר אָהַבְתָּ אֶת יִצְחָק — "Your Son, Your Only Son, Whom You Love, Isaac" (v. 2) — The Fourfold Identification**

Verse 2 is one of the most rhetorically intense verses in the Bible. The divine command intensifies through four successive, escalating identifications before the name is finally spoken:

אֶת בִּנְךָ
(et binkha — "your son")
אֶת יְחִידְךָ
(et yechidkha — "your only son")
אֲשֶׁר אָהַבְתָּ
(asher ahavta — "whom you love")
אֶת יִצְחָק
(et Yitschaq — "Isaac")

The structure is that of a tragic recognition scene delayed to its last possible moment: the reader knows, Abraham knows, and the command takes its full weight only when the beloved name lands at the end. This rhetorical pattern — delaying the name through three intensifying qualifications — is unique in the Pentateuch and has no parallel in its affective power. The formal translation preserves each element in sequence without compression: "your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love" would lose the climactic placement of the name; instead LOGOS renders the Hebrew order precisely: "your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love" — placing Isaac after "only son" and before "whom you love" to keep the name in its penultimate position as the text has it.

The word יְחִידְךָ (yechidkha, "your only one / your solitary one") is from the root יָחִיד (yachid), meaning "only, alone, solitary." It is the same word used in Psalm 22:20 ("my only life / my solitary one") and Psalm 35:17 ("my only life"). Its use here carries a resonance beyond mere arithmetic only-ness: it speaks to the utter singularity, the irreplaceability, of Isaac in Abraham's life. The LXX renders: τὸν υἱόν σου τὸν ἀγαπητόν, ὃν ἠγάπησας, τὸν Ισαακ (ton huion sou ton agapeton, hon egapesas, ton Isaak — "your son, your beloved, whom you loved, Isaac"), using ἀγαπητός (agapetos, "beloved, only-beloved") for יְחִיד (yachid). This LXX rendering of יְחִיד as ἀγαπητός is significant: the identical Greek word is used of Jesus at his baptism (Matthew 3:17: "This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased") and at the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:5). The verbal echo between the Aqedah's LXX and the Gospel accounts of the divine voice at Jesus' baptism is unmistakable and theologially freighted.

The command: לֶךְ לְךָ אֶל אֶרֶץ הַמֹּרִיָּה (lekh lekha el erets haMoriyyah — "go to the land of Moriah") employs the same idiom לֶךְ לְךָ (lekh lekha — "go, yourself / go forth") used at the original call of Abraham in Genesis 12:1 ("Go from your country"). The Aqedah is structurally framed as a second call — the first call inaugurated the Abrahamic journey; this second call is its supreme test. The formal translation renders the idiom simply as "go" (as standard English requires), but the structural echo with 12:1 is noted here.

**3. הַר אַחַד אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיךָ — "One of the Mountains of Which I Shall Tell You" (v. 2)**

The divine command is deliberately vague about which mountain — אַחַד הֶהָרִים (achad heharim, "one of the mountains") — just as the call of Genesis 12:1 was vague about the destination ("the land that I will show you"). The same divine withholding of full information drives the narrative: Abraham must move on trust before the specifics are revealed. The formal translation preserves this deliberate vagueness, rendering "one of the mountains of which I shall tell you" rather than any harmonizing reading.

**4. וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר — "Abraham Rose Early in the Morning" (v. 3)**

The idiom וַיַּשְׁכֵּם בַּבֹּקֶר (vayyashkem babboqer, "and he rose early in the morning") is used elsewhere of Abraham's urgent rising to send Hagar away (Genesis 21:14) and is a marker of resolute, immediate obedience. Abraham does not deliberate or delay; he rises early and acts. The formal translation preserves this verb and idiom precisely.

**5. אֲנִי וְהַנַּעַר נֵלְכָה עַד כֹּה וְנִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה וְנָשׁוּבָה אֲלֵיכֶם — "I and the Boy Will Go Over There and Worship and Come Back to You" (v. 5)**

This verse has generated enormous interpretive discussion. Abraham says to the servants:

אֲנִי וְהַנַּעַר נֵלְכָה עַד כֹּה
(ani vehannaʿar nelkha ad koh — "I and the boy will go over there")
וְנִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה
(venishtatchweh — "and we will worship")
וְנָשׁוּבָה אֲלֵיכֶם
(venashuvah aleikhem — "and we will come back to you")

The verbs נֵלְכָה, נִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה, and נָשׁוּבָה are all first person plural cohortatives — "we will go," "we will worship," "we will return." Both Abraham and Isaac are grammatically subjects of all three verbs, including the return. Is Abraham deceiving his servants? Is this a deliberate concealment of his intent? Or does Abraham, by faith, genuinely believe that both of them will return — perhaps expecting resurrection (as the writer of Hebrews 11:19 suggests: "He considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead, from which, figuratively speaking, he did receive him back")? The text is silent on Abraham's inner psychology, and the formal translation preserves that silence, rendering the first person plural exactly as the Hebrew has it: "I and the boy will go over there and worship and come back to you." The ambiguity is the text's own.

**6. אֱלֹהִים יִרְאֶה לּוֹ הַשֶּׂה לְעֹלָה — "God Will Provide for Himself the Lamb for a Burnt Offering" (v. 8)**

This is the theological heart of the narrative, spoken by Abraham to Isaac before the full import of the situation is known to either Isaac or the reader:

אֱלֹהִים יִרְאֶה לּוֹ הַשֶּׂה לְעֹלָה
(Elohim yireh lo hasseh laolah — "God will see/provide for himself the lamb for a burnt offering")

The verb יִרְאֶה (yireh) is from רָאָה (ra'ah, "to see"). The idiomatic expression יִרְאֶה לּוֹ (yireh lo, "he will see to it for himself / he will provide for himself") is the basis for the place name in verse 14: יְהוָה יִרְאֶה (YHWH-yireh, "the LORD will see / the LORD will provide"). The verb "to see" and "to provide" are the same word in Hebrew: YHWH's seeing is his providing. This is not evasion by Abraham — it is either an act of faith expressing trust that God will indeed supply what is needed (as the outcome proves), or a deliberate double-meaning statement that operates on two levels simultaneously (Abraham provides the answer to Isaac's question without yet fully knowing how the answer will be fulfilled). The formal translation renders "God will provide for himself the lamb for a burnt offering" — preserving the reflexive לּוֹ ("for himself"), which many translations lose (NIV: "God himself will provide"), in order to maintain the verbal connection with verse 14. The phrase "for himself" is not redundant; it signals that the provision is entirely God's initiative, from within his own resources.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders: ὁ θεὸς ὄψεται ἑαυτῷ πρόβατον εἰς ὁλοκάρπωσιν (ho theos opsetai heauto probaton eis holokarposis — "God will see for himself a sheep for a whole burnt offering"), preserving the reflexive ἑαυτῷ (heauto, "for himself") exactly as the Hebrew has it.

**7. וַיַּעֲקֹד — "He Bound" (v. 9) — The Aqedah**

The word from which the entire episode takes its name — הָעֲקֵדָה (ha'Aqedah, "the Binding") — appears only once in the Bible, here in verse 9:

וַיַּעֲקֹד אֶת יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ
(vayyaaqod et Yitschaq beno — "and he bound Isaac his son")

The verb עָקַד (aqad) means to bind the limbs together, specifically the binding of an animal's legs before sacrifice. It is a hapax legomenon in the Hebrew Bible — used only here. The formal translation renders "he bound Isaac his son," placing the name and the relationship ("his son") in full view, as the Hebrew demands.

Isaac's silence during the binding and the preparation of the altar has been the subject of profound meditation in both Jewish and Christian traditions. The text says nothing of protest or struggle. The Aqedah tradition in later Judaism (particularly in the Targums and in 4Q225 from Qumran/Dead Sea Scrolls) increasingly reads Isaac as a willing, knowing participant who actively cooperates with his father. The MT text itself maintains silence on Isaac's inner state, and the formal translation follows that silence.

**8. מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה — "The Angel of the LORD" (vv. 11, 15) — Divine Mediation**

The messenger who intervenes is designated מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה (mal'akh YHWH, "the angel of the LORD" or "the messenger of the LORD"). The same designation is used of the angel who appeared to Hagar in Genesis 16 and 21, and throughout the patriarchal narratives. The double address "Abraham, Abraham!" (אַבְרָהָם אַבְרָהָם, Avraham Avraham) in verse 11 is a rhetorical urgency marker — the doubled name signals crisis and a summons requiring immediate response. The same pattern appears at the burning bush (Exodus 3:4: "Moses, Moses!") and in Samuel's call (1 Samuel 3:10). The formal translation preserves the doubled name without compression.

**9. כִּי עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי כִּי יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה — "Now I Know That You Fear God" (v. 12)**

The angel's words after staying Abraham's hand:

כִּי עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי
(ki attah yadaʿti — "for now I know")
כִּי יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה
(ki yere Elohim attah — "that you are a fearer of God / that you fear God")

The phrase "now I know" has generated theological discussion about divine omniscience: does God learn something new? The formal translation renders the text as it stands without theological resolution. The phrase "fear God" (יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים, yere Elohim) is the central virtue of Genesis 20 (Abimelech's question: "Is there no fear of God in this place?") and of Job (Job 1:1: "a man who feared God"). It is the integrating virtue of the wisdom tradition: "the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom" (Proverbs 9:10). Here Abraham is formally certified as one who fears God. The formal translation renders "you fear God" — a verbal phrase rather than the noun phrase "God-fearer" — to preserve the relational and active character of the Hebrew.

**10. יְהוָה יִרְאֶה — "The LORD Will Provide" / "YHWH-Yireh" (v. 14)**

The great naming of the place:

וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא יְהוָה יִרְאֶה
(vayyiqra' Avraham shem hammaqom hahu YHWH yireh)
"So Abraham called the name of that place, 'The LORD will provide'"

The name יְהוָה יִרְאֶה (YHWH-yireh, "the LORD will see / the LORD will provide") takes up the verb יִרְאֶה from Abraham's faith-statement in verse 8 ("God will provide for himself the lamb") and applies it as the permanent name of the place where the providing happened. The seeing and the providing are the same act: God's attention to a situation is his provision for it.

The aetiological saying that follows:

אֲשֶׁר יֵאָמֵר הַיּוֹם בְּהַר יְהוָה יֵרָאֶה
(asher ye'amer hayyom behar YHWH yera'eh)
"As it is said to this day, 'On the mount of the LORD it shall be provided/seen'"

The verb יֵרָאֶה (yera'eh) is the Niphal (passive) of רָאָה — "it will be seen" or "it will be provided." The Niphal of ra'ah can mean either "to be seen / to appear" or (as a functional passive of the Hiphil provident sense) "to be provided." The formal translation renders "it shall be provided" for clarity, while noting that the Hebrew verb is literally "it shall be seen" — and that the seeing/providing ambiguity is deliberate and irreducible. The place tradition links the Moriah of the Aqedah with the threshing floor of Araunah, the site of Solomon's Temple (2 Chronicles 3:1: "Solomon began to build the house of the LORD in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah").

**11. כִּי בֵרֵךְ אֲבָרֶכְךָ — "I Will Surely Bless You" (v. 17) — The Infinitive Absolute Construction**

The divine oath of verses 16–18 employs the emphatic Hebrew infinitive absolute construction:

כִּי בָרֵךְ אֲבָרֶכְךָ
(ki varekh avarekha — "for blessing I will bless you")
וְהַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת זַרְעֲךָ
(veharbeh arbeh et zarakha — "and multiplying I will multiply your offspring")

The doubled form (infinitive absolute + finite verb of the same root) is the standard Hebrew emphatic construction: "I will surely / I will certainly / I will indeed." The formal translation renders "I will surely bless you" and "I will surely multiply your offspring," which captures the emphatic force without the awkwardness of a literal "blessing I will bless."

**12. וְיִרַשׁ זַרְעֲךָ אֵת שַׁעַר אֹיְבָיו — "Your Offspring Shall Possess the Gate of His Enemies" (v. 17)**

The idiom שַׁעַר אֹיְבָיו (sha'ar oyevav, "the gate of his enemies") refers to the city gate as the seat of civic authority, military strength, and judicial power in the ancient Near East. To "possess the gate" of one's enemies is to take complete control of their cities — a vivid military and political metaphor for total victory. The same promise recurs at 24:60 (spoken over Rebekah: "may your offspring possess the gate of his enemies"). The formal translation preserves the idiom rather than rendering it by interpretation ("defeat your enemies" would lose the specific gate imagery). The shift from the plural "offspring" (זַרְעֲךָ, zarʿakha) to the singular "his enemies" (אֹיְבָיו, oyevav) is a grammatical feature of Hebrew זֶרַע (zeraʿ, "offspring/seed"), which is a collective noun taking either singular or plural agreement — a feature the NT (Galatians 3:16) exploits in Paul's argument about "offspring" versus "offsprings."

**13. וְהִתְבָּרֲכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ — "Shall Be Blessed" (v. 18) — Hithpael or Niphal?**

The verb וְהִתְבָּרֲכוּ (vehitbarakhu) in verse 18 is a Hithpael form of בָּרַךְ (barakh, "to bless") — the Hithpael being the reflexive-reciprocal stem. The precise sense is contested:

- **Hithpael reflexive**: "all the nations of the earth shall bless themselves by/through your offspring" — meaning they will use Abraham's offspring as the standard of blessing ("May you be like Abraham's seed!")
- **Hithpael passive** (the Hithpael occasionally functions passively in Biblical Hebrew): "all the nations of the earth shall be blessed through your offspring" — meaning the blessing actually flows to the nations
- The parallel passage in Genesis 12:3 uses the Niphal form וְנִבְרְכוּ (venivrekhu) — which is the standard passive: "shall be blessed." The shift from Niphal (12:3) to Hithpael (22:18) may be stylistic or may carry a distinct nuance.

Paul in Galatians 3:8 cites the promise in its passive sense: "In you shall all the nations be blessed" (ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν σοί πάντα τὰ ἔθνη — using a future passive). The LXX of 22:18 (Vaticanus) renders: ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν τῷ σπέρματί σου πάντα τὰ ἔθνη τῆς γῆς (eneulogethesontai en to spermati sou panta ta ethne tes ges — "all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in your offspring") — reading the Hithpael as a passive, which is the reading Paul adopts.

The formal translation renders "shall be blessed" (following ESV, NASB, and the LXX/Paul tradition), acknowledging the passive sense as the dominant interpretive trajectory while noting the Hithpael form.

**14. The Nahor Genealogy — Why Here? (vv. 20–24)**

The genealogical appendix at the end of the Aqedah narrative — listing the children and grandchildren of Abraham's brother Nahor — is structurally significant far beyond its apparent modesty. Its final item is the key:

וּבְתוּאֵל יָלַד אֶת רִבְקָה
(uVetuel yalad et Rivqah — "and Bethuel fathered Rebekah")

Rebekah will become Isaac's wife (Genesis 24) — and so this genealogy, placed immediately after the Aqedah, subtly prepares the narrative: Isaac, the son who was almost offered and was returned to his father, will now receive the wife of God's appointment. The chapter that begins with the most extreme threat to the line of promise closes with the quiet assurance that the line continues. The formal translation renders the genealogy in full, preserving all eight names of Milcah's sons and the four sons of Reumah.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 22

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 17–18 (GEN_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The LXX of Genesis 22 is largely consistent with the MT but carries several noteworthy variants and expansions:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 22:1 | נִסָּה (nissah, "tested") | ἐπείραζεν (epeiradzen, "was testing/tempting") | "tested" | LXX uses the same verb as NT "tempt" — theologically fraught; MT "tested" preserved |
| 22:2 | יְחִידְךָ (yechidkha, "your only son") | τὸν ἀγαπητόν (ton agapeton, "your beloved") | "your only son" | LXX's ἀγαπητός echoes the divine voice at Jesus' baptism (Matt 3:17); formal translation follows MT |
| 22:2 | הַמֹּרִיָּה (haMoriyyah, "Moriah") | τὴν ὑψηλήν (ten hypselen, "the high/lofty [land]") | "Moriah" | LXX appears to have read or interpreted differently, rendering "the high land" — possibly a different Hebrew Vorlage or an interpretive rendering; formal translation follows MT |
| 22:8 | הַשֶּׂה (hasseh, "the lamb/sheep") | πρόβατον (probaton, "sheep") | "the lamb" | Agreement; formal translation specifies "lamb" to preserve the expected sacrificial animal |
| 22:13 | אַחַר (achar, "behind him") | ἓν (hen, "one") | "behind him" | Significant textual variant: MT reads "and behold, behind him (אַחַר) a ram"; LXX reads "and behold one (ἓν) ram." Many MSS of the Hebrew read אֶחָד (echad, "one") — the consonants אחד and אחר differ only in ד/ר, which are easily confused. The Samaritan Pentateuch and several manuscripts support אֶחָד ("one ram"). The formal translation follows the MT reading "behind him" but notes the variant |
| 22:14 | יֵרָאֶה (yera'eh, "shall be seen/provided") | ὤφθη (ophthe, "appeared / was seen") | "it shall be provided" | LXX uses aorist passive of ὁράω — "the LORD appeared / was seen on the mountain" — reading the Niphal as a statement of divine appearance rather than provision |
| 22:18 | וְהִתְבָּרֲכוּ (vehitbarakhu, Hithpael — "shall bless themselves / shall be blessed") | ἐνευλογηθήσονται (eneulogethesontai, future passive — "shall be blessed") | "shall be blessed" | LXX reads passive; this is the form Paul cites in Gal 3:8; formal translation follows LXX/Paul |

The most significant variant in this chapter is at verse 13, where MT reads אַחַר ("behind him") and the LXX reads ἓν ("one") — reflecting a probable Hebrew Vorlage of אֶחָד ("one") rather than אַחַר ("behind him"). The ד/ר confusion in the square script is well attested, and the reading "one ram caught in the thicket" (vs. "a ram caught in the thicket behind him") is supported by the Syriac Peshitta as well. The formal translation follows the MT as preserved in the Leningrad Codex while noting this important variant.

The LXX rendering of יְחִיד (yachid, "only/solitary") as ἀγαπητός (agapetos, "beloved") at verse 2 represents one of the most theologically consequential translation decisions in the entire Septuagint. By rendering the Hebrew "only son" with the Greek "beloved son," the LXX translators (and/or the tradition they represent) created a verbal bridge that would be activated centuries later in the Gospel accounts of the divine voice at Jesus' baptism: "This is my beloved Son" (Matt 3:17 // Mark 1:11 // Luke 3:22). Whether this is a deliberate typological rendering by the LXX translators or a felicitous coincidence of translation, the theological resonance is profound: the "beloved son" Isaac carried the wood of his own intended sacrifice up Mount Moriah; the "beloved Son" Jesus carried his cross.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 22 — Three Movements**

The chapter divides into three clearly demarcated narrative movements:

1. **vv. 1–10**: The test — the journey, the ascent, the binding, the raised knife
2. **vv. 11–19**: The provision — the angel's intervention, the ram, the naming, the oath and blessing, the return to Beer-sheba
3. **vv. 20–24**: The genealogical coda — the Nahor family list culminating in Rebekah

The first movement is governed by the verb הָלַךְ (halakh, "to go, to walk"), used repeatedly to trace Abraham and Isaac's journey toward the mountain. The second movement is governed by the verb רָאָה (ra'ah, "to see / to provide"), which appears in Abraham's faith-statement (v. 8), the place naming (v. 14 twice), and the aetiology. The third movement is a quiet genealogical close that opens the next narrative horizon.

**The Aqedah and the Theology of the Test**

Genesis 22 stands as the supreme test of the Abrahamic narrative because it pits the two fundamental divine commitments against each other: the command to offer Isaac, and the promise that "through Isaac shall your offspring be named" (Genesis 21:12). For Abraham to obey the command would appear to destroy the promise. The narrative's resolution — God providing the ram, the promise renewed in the most solemn possible terms (sworn by the divine Name) — reveals that the test was never about whether Isaac would die, but about whether Abraham's trust in God was absolute. The Hebrews 11:17–19 interpretation — that Abraham "considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead" — offers one theological reading of Abraham's inner state, consistent with the first person plural verbs of verse 5 ("we will come back to you").

**The Moriah-Jerusalem Connection**

The identification of "the land of Moriah" (v. 2) with Mount Moriah in Jerusalem — the site of Solomon's Temple and, in the broader tradition, the site of the later Temple Mount — rests on 2 Chronicles 3:1: "Solomon began to build the house of the LORD in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah, where the LORD had appeared to David his father." The etymological link (Moriah from ra'ah, "to see/appear") and the theological link (the place where God provided and appeared) tie the Aqedah, the Davidic threshing floor, and the Temple together in the Chronicler's reading. The formal translation renders "land of Moriah" without interpretive expansion, preserving the text as the Leningrad Codex presents it.

**The Divine Name Pattern in Genesis 22**

The chapter uses both Elohim and YHWH with deliberate structural purpose:
- הָאֱלֹהִים (ha'Elohim, "the God") dominates the test section (vv. 1, 3, 8, 9, 12) — God as the universal, sovereign tester
- יְהוָה (YHWH) appears in the messenger's title (vv. 11, 15), the place name YHWH-yireh (v. 14), and the divine oath formula "declares the LORD" (v. 16) — YHWH as the covenant God who provides and swears by his own name
- אֱלֹהִים (Elohim without article) appears in Abraham's faith-statement to Isaac (v. 8: "God will provide")

This alternation is not simply a source-critical datum; within the narrative as it stands in the Leningrad Codex, it creates a theological movement from the universal sovereign (testing) to the covenant name (providing and swearing). The formal translation maintains this distinction throughout.
